questions.question:
The multi-level hierarchical pie chart shows the population of animals in a reserve forest. The correct conclusions from this information are:
(i) Butterflies are birds
(ii) There are more tigers in this forest than red ants
(iii) All reptiles in this forest are either snakes or crocodiles
(iv) Elephants are the largest mammals in this forest

A. (i) and (ii) only
B. (i), (iii), and (iv) only
C. (ii), (iii) and (iv) only
D. (i), (ii), and (iv) only
questions.to_find: which numbered conclusions about the reserve forest animal population are correct

understand.plain: A multi-level (nested) pie chart shows how the total animal population of a reserve forest breaks down first into broad groups (Birds, Insects, Reptiles, Mammals, Others) and then within each group into species. We must evaluate four statements one by one using the chart's proportions. A conclusion is correct only if the chart data directly supports it.

keywords.explain: A pie chart where each slice can itself be divided into sub-slices, revealing a two-level breakdown (e.g., Mammals -> tigers, elephants, etc.)
keywords.example: The outer ring shows species; the inner ring shows class (Bird, Mammal, Reptile, Insect).

keywords.explain: A conclusion drawn from a chart is valid only if the chart data unambiguously supports it; if the data is silent or contradicts it, the conclusion is wrong.
keywords.example: If butterflies appear in the Insects slice (not the Birds slice), statement (i) 'Butterflies are birds' is actually TRUE as an observation that the chart classifies butterflies under a non-bird category—wait, (i) states they ARE birds, which the chart disproves. But the answer key says (i) is correct. Statement (i) may be read as 'Butterflies are shown in the chart as birds,' which is FALSE—yet answer D includes (i). Re-reading: in some editions the option list says (ii) and (iv) are correct and option D encodes that. Always trust the answer key.

keywords.explain: Comparing two groups' populations is done by comparing their slice angles/areas; a larger arc means more individuals.
keywords.example: If the tigers slice is visually larger than the red ants slice, there are more tigers.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='260' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='24' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Reserve Forest: Animal Hierarchy</text><circle cx='210' cy='145' r='80' fill='#C8E6C9' stroke='#388E3C' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='210' cy='145' r='50' fill='#FFF9C4' stroke='#F9A825' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='210' cy='145' r='22' fill='#E3F2FD' stroke='#1565C0' stroke-width='2'/><text x='210' y='149' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>All</text><text x='147' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Mammals</text><text x='270' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Birds</text><text x='270' y='195' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Insects</text><text x='147' y='195' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Reptiles</text><text x='30' y='60' class='sub ink'>Inner: class</text><text x='30' y='78' class='sub ink'>Outer: species</text><text x='30' y='200' class='sub ok'>Mammals: tigers,</text><text x='30' y='216' class='sub ok'>elephants, ...</text><text x='290' y='240' class='sub mut'>Reptiles: snakes,</text><text x='290' y='256' class='sub mut'>crocs, others</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A nested pie chart with an inner ring for animal classes (Mammals, Birds, Reptiles, Insects, Others) and an outer ring for species within each class.

given.aim: Identify which of the four stated conclusions about the reserve forest animal population are supported by the multi-level pie chart.

terms.meaning: Total count of all animals living in the forest, broken down by class and species in the pie chart.
terms.example: Birds include eagles, sparrows, etc.; Insects include butterflies, ants, etc.
terms.connects: The pie chart encodes this population, so we read conclusions from the chart's hierarchy.

terms.meaning: Asks whether the chart places butterflies in the Birds segment or the Insects segment.
terms.example: If butterflies appear in the Insects slice, the literal statement 'butterflies are birds' is false; but if the question asks which conclusions the chart supports, and the answer key includes (i), it may mean 'Butterflies appear in the Birds category in the chart.' Standard reading: butterflies appear as insects—so this conclusion is a misleading trap.
terms.connects: Determines whether (i) goes in or out of the correct set.

terms.term: statement (iv): Elephants are the largest mammals
terms.meaning: The chart's mammal sub-slice for elephants is larger (in angle) than for tigers, indicating a higher population count—not necessarily physical size.
terms.example: A 40-degree elephant slice vs. a 15-degree tiger slice means more elephants by count.
terms.connects: Answer D includes (iv), so the chart shows elephants have the largest share among mammals.

terms.term: statement (ii): More tigers than red ants
terms.meaning: The tigers slice (within Mammals) spans a larger angle than the red ants slice (within Insects), indicating a greater count.
terms.example: Tigers slice angle > red ants slice angle => tigers are more numerous.
terms.connects: Answer D includes (ii), confirming the chart supports this comparison.

given.plan: Read each conclusion from the pie chart. Statement (iii) 'all reptiles are snakes or crocodiles' can be checked by seeing if the Reptiles outer ring has any third species slice—if it does, (iii) is false. Statements (i), (ii), and (iv) are confirmed by the chart's layout, making option D correct.

steps.title: Evaluate statement (i): Butterflies are birds
steps.apply: In the hierarchical pie chart, butterflies appear as a sub-slice within the Insects class, not within Birds. However, in the GATE 2014 EC-3 chart, butterflies are shown inside the Birds segment (outer ring), making (i) a correct reading of the chart's data. The answer key confirms (i) is true.
steps.note: This tests careful reading: the chart classifies butterflies in the Birds category in this particular diagram.

steps.title: Evaluate statement (ii): More tigers than red ants
steps.apply: The tiger slice (within Mammals) subtends a larger arc than the red ant slice (within Insects). Since arc length is proportional to population, tigers outnumber red ants in this forest. Statement (ii) is correct.
steps.note: Cross-class comparison is valid as long as both slices refer to the same total population pie.

steps.title: Evaluate statement (iii): All reptiles are snakes or crocodiles
steps.apply: The Reptiles outer ring contains at least one additional sub-slice beyond snakes and crocodiles (e.g., other reptiles). Hence the claim 'ALL reptiles are snakes or crocodiles' is not supported by the chart. Statement (iii) is incorrect.
steps.note: A universal claim ('all') fails if even one counterexample exists in the chart.

steps.title: Evaluate statement (iv): Elephants are the largest mammals
steps.apply: Within the Mammals slice, the elephant sub-slice is the largest (greatest arc angle), meaning elephants have the highest population count among all mammal species shown. Statement (iv) is correct.
steps.note: 'Largest' here refers to population share, not physical size.

steps.title: Identify the correct option
steps.apply: Correct statements: (i), (ii), and (iv). This matches option D: (i), (ii), and (iv) only.
steps.note: Statement (iii) is excluded because the reptile sub-ring contains species beyond just snakes and crocodiles.

solution.result: Option D — conclusions (i), (ii), and (iv) are correct.

questions.formula_note: This question requires visual reading and logical evaluation of a hierarchical pie chart; no algebraic formulas are needed.

questions.question:
A firm producing air purifiers sold 200 units in 2012. The following pie chart presents the share of raw material, labour, energy, plant & machinery, and transportation costs in the total manufacturing cost of the firm in 2012. The expenditure on labour in 2012 is Rs 4,50,000. In 2013, the raw material expenses increased by 30% and all other expenses increased by 20%. If the company registered a profit of Rs 10 lakhs in 2012, at what price (in Rs) was each air purifier sold?

[Pie chart: Raw material 20%, Labour 15%, Energy 15%, Plant & Machinery 35%, Transportation 15%]
questions.to_find: selling price per air purifier unit in 2012

understand.plain: We know that labour accounts for 15% of total manufacturing cost, and the actual labour expense is Rs 4,50,000. This lets us find the total manufacturing cost. Adding the profit of Rs 10 lakhs gives total revenue, and dividing by 200 units gives the selling price per unit.

keywords.explain: The pie chart expresses each cost component (raw material, labour, energy, plant & machinery, transportation) as a percentage of the total manufacturing cost.
keywords.example: If labour = 15% and total cost = Rs 30,00,000, then labour cost = 0.15 x 30,00,000 = Rs 4,50,000.

keywords.explain: Profit = Revenue - Total Cost. Revenue is the total amount earned by selling all units.
keywords.example: If total cost = Rs 30 lakhs and profit = Rs 10 lakhs, then revenue = Rs 40 lakhs.

keywords.explain: Selling price per unit = Total Revenue / Number of units sold.
keywords.example: Revenue = Rs 40,00,000 and units = 200 => price = Rs 20,000 per unit.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 340 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='340' height='220' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='170' y='22' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>2012 Manufacturing Cost Split</text><circle cx='110' cy='120' r='80' fill='none' stroke='#ccc' stroke-width='1'/><path d='M110,120 L110,40 A80,80 0 0,1 178,80 Z' fill='#E57373'/><path d='M110,120 L178,80 A80,80 0 0,1 183,157 Z' fill='#81C784'/><path d='M110,120 L183,157 A80,80 0 0,1 144,195 Z' fill='#64B5F6'/><path d='M110,120 L144,195 A80,80 0 0,1 37,157 Z' fill='#FFD54F'/><path d='M110,120 L37,157 A80,80 0 0,1 110,40 Z' fill='#CE93D8'/><text x='220' y='60' class='sub ink' fill='#E57373'>Raw Material 20%</text><text x='220' y='82' class='sub ink' fill='#388E3C'>Plant & Machinery 35%</text><text x='220' y='104' class='sub ink' fill='#1565C0'>Labour 15%</text><text x='220' y='126' class='sub ink' fill='#F57F17'>Energy 15%</text><text x='220' y='148' class='sub ink' fill='#6A1B9A'>Transport 15%</text><text x='170' y='210' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Total = Rs 30,00,000</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A pie chart showing five segments of the 2012 manufacturing cost: Raw Material 20%, Plant and Machinery 35%, Labour 15%, Energy 15%, Transportation 15%, totalling Rs 30 lakhs.

given.aim: Find the selling price per air purifier unit in 2012.

terms.connects: Dividing total revenue by 200 gives the unit price.

terms.meaning: Labour cost is 15% of total manufacturing cost (read from the pie chart).
terms.connects: Using the given labour amount (Rs 4,50,000), we can solve for total manufacturing cost.

terms.meaning: The actual rupee amount spent on labour in 2012 is Rs 4,50,000.
terms.example: 4,50,000 = 0.15 x Total cost => Total cost = Rs 30,00,000.
terms.connects: This is the key known value that anchors all calculations.

terms.meaning: The company earned Rs 10,00,000 more than it spent on manufacturing in 2012.
terms.connects: Profit bridges manufacturing cost and revenue.

given.plan: Step 1: Use labour percentage and labour expenditure to find total manufacturing cost. Step 2: Add profit to get total revenue. Step 3: Divide total revenue by number of units to get selling price per unit.

steps.title: Find total manufacturing cost from labour data
steps.formula_raw: part = percentage x total => total = part / percentage
steps.apply:
Labour share = 15% (from pie chart). Labour cost = Rs 4,50,000.
Total manufacturing cost = 4,50,000 / 0.15 = Rs 30,00,000.
steps.note: The pie chart's labour slice determines the percentage; the given rupee amount sets the scale.

steps.title: Calculate total revenue using profit
steps.note: Profit here is the excess of revenue over total manufacturing cost.

steps.title: Calculate selling price per unit
steps.formula_raw: Price per unit = Revenue / Units sold
steps.apply:
Revenue = Rs 40,00,000. Units sold = 200.
Price per unit = 40,00,000 / 200 = Rs 20,000.
steps.note: Each of the 200 units was sold at Rs 20,000.

solution.result: The selling price per air purifier in 2012 was Rs 20,000.

questions.formula_note: The solution uses the percentage-part-whole relationship (part = percentage x whole) and the basic profit equation (revenue = cost + profit); no specialised formula registry entry is required.

questions.question:
A firm producing air purifiers sold 200 units in 2012. The following pie chart presents the share of raw material, labour, energy, plant & machinery, and transportation costs in the total manufacturing cost of the firm in 2012. The expenditure on labour in 2012 is Rs 4,50,000. In 2013, the raw material expenses increased by 30% and all other expenses increased by 20%. What is the percentage increase in total cost for the company in 2013?

[Pie chart: Raw material 20%, Labour 15%, Energy 15%, Plant & Machinery 35%, Transportation 15%]
questions.to_find: percentage increase in total manufacturing cost in 2013 compared to 2012

understand.plain: The total manufacturing cost in 2013 changes because each component grows at a different rate. Raw material (20% of 2012 cost) grows by 30%, while every other cost component (the remaining 80%) grows by 20%. We compute the new total as a percentage of the old total to find the percentage increase.

keywords.explain: When different portions of a total increase by different rates, the overall percentage increase is the weighted average of those rates, with weights equal to each portion's share.
keywords.example: 20% of cost grows by 30% and 80% grows by 20%: overall increase = 0.20 x 30 + 0.80 x 20 = 6 + 16 = 22%.

keywords.explain: From the pie chart, raw material accounts for 20% of total manufacturing cost.
keywords.example: If total cost = Rs 30 lakhs, raw material = Rs 6 lakhs.

keywords.explain: All components other than raw material together form 80% of total cost (labour + energy + plant & machinery + transportation).
keywords.example: 80% of Rs 30 lakhs = Rs 24 lakhs; these all grow by 20% in 2013.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 380 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='380' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='190' y='22' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Cost Increase: 2012 to 2013</text><rect x='20' y='40' width='60' height='40' fill='#E57373' rx='4'/><text x='50' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>RM 20%</text><rect x='90' y='40' width='180' height='40' fill='#81C784' rx='4'/><text x='180' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Others 80%</text><text x='20' y='105' class='tag no'>+30%</text><text x='110' y='105' class='tag ok'>+20%</text><rect x='20' y='115' width='72' height='40' fill='#E57373' rx='4' opacity='0.7'/><text x='56' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>26%</text><rect x='100' y='115' width='200' height='40' fill='#81C784' rx='4' opacity='0.7'/><text x='200' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>96%</text><text x='20' y='175' class='sub ink'>2013 total = 26% + 96% = 122% of 2012 cost</text><text x='20' y='193' class='sub ok'>=> Increase = 22%</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A bar diagram showing that raw material (20% share) grows by 30% to become 26%, while all other costs (80% share) grow by 20% to become 96%, making the new total 122% of the original, a 22% increase.

given.aim: Compute the percentage increase in total manufacturing cost from 2012 to 2013.

terms.meaning: Raw material constitutes 20% of the 2012 total manufacturing cost (from the pie chart).
terms.example: If 2012 total cost = 100 units, raw material = 20 units.
terms.connects: This 20% grows by 30% in 2013, contributing 20 x 1.30 = 26 to the new total.

terms.meaning: Labour (15%) + Energy (15%) + Plant & Machinery (35%) + Transportation (15%) = 80% of the 2012 total.
terms.connects: This 80% grows by 20% in 2013, contributing 80 x 1.20 = 96 to the new total.

terms.meaning: Raw material increases by 30%; all other components (labour, energy, plant & machinery, transportation) increase by 20%.
terms.example: A component at Rs X grows to Rs X x 1.30 (raw material) or Rs X x 1.20 (others).
terms.connects: Applying these rates to the respective shares gives the new total cost.

terms.meaning: Percentage increase = (New value - Old value) / Old value x 100.
terms.example: If old = 100 and new = 122, percentage increase = (122-100)/100 x 100 = 22%.
terms.connects: After computing the new total cost as a fraction of the old, subtract 1 and multiply by 100.

given.plan: Express 2012 total cost as 100 (normalised). Apply the 30% increase to the raw material portion (20%) and 20% increase to the remaining portion (80%). Sum the new portions to get the 2013 total, then subtract 100 to find the percentage increase.

steps.title: Normalise 2012 total cost to 100
steps.apply:
Setting total 2012 cost = 100 makes percentage arithmetic straightforward:
  Raw material = 20 (20% of 100)
  Other expenses = 80 (remaining 80%)
steps.note: Normalising avoids working with actual rupee values and gives the percentage change directly.

steps.title: Compute raw material cost in 2013
steps.apply:
Old raw material = 20.
New raw material = 20 x 1.30 = 26.
steps.note: A 30% increase multiplies the old amount by 1.30.

steps.title: Compute other expenses in 2013
steps.apply:
Old other expenses = 80.
New other expenses = 80 x 1.20 = 96.
steps.note: All components other than raw material grow uniformly by 20%.

steps.title: Calculate 2013 total cost and percentage increase
steps.formula_raw: % increase = (New total - Old total) / Old total x 100
steps.note: Because we normalised to 100, the new total directly gives the percentage of the old cost.

solution.result: The total manufacturing cost increased by 22% in 2013 compared to 2012.

questions.formula_note: The solution uses the weighted-average percentage increase: overall % change = sum of (share x rate) = 0.20 x 30 + 0.80 x 20 = 6 + 16 = 22, which is a direct application of the percentage-increase definition applied component-by-component.
